%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.1 Complex Numbers — Foundation Review

> **Section Objective:** Build the notation and geometric intuition for complex numbers that every signals-and-systems topic depends on.

This section is not abstract history — it is the foundation for phasors, frequency response, and Laplace and Fourier transforms. Complex numbers enter engineering because they **simplify derivations**, even when the original problem and the final answer are entirely real. The textbook's historical route analogy captures this: sometimes the fastest path between two real points passes through complex territory.

By the end of this section you should be able to read \(z = a + jb\) on the complex plane, switch fluently between rectangular and polar form, and avoid the high-frequency exam trap of confusing the imaginary **part** \(b\) with the imaginary **term** \(jb\). These distinctions will reappear every time you work with \(e^{j\theta}\) in signal analysis.

## 1. Why Complex Numbers Are Worth Using

Many engineering problems begin with real quantities — voltages, currents, displacements — and end with real results. Yet the derivation in between is often far shorter and cleaner when complex numbers are used as an intermediary tool.

This is not a philosophical claim about physical reality. It is a statement about **mathematical efficiency**: the complex-number route can be shorter even though the destination is the same real-valued answer.

### KEY EXAM INSIGHT

A method that introduces \(j\) partway through a derivation does **not** imply the final answer must be complex. The presence of \(j\) mid-solution is a tool choice, not a statement about the answer.

> **Common Trap:** Do not assume complex numbers are only relevant when the final answer is complex-valued. This misconception causes avoidable errors on exams.

This efficiency argument is exactly why \(e^{j\theta}\) appears so prominently in signals and systems: it compresses trigonometric manipulations into clean algebraic steps.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Complex numbers act as a shortcut: the problem starts and ends in the real-number region, but passing through the complex region can make the derivation significantly shorter.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215695559-2127.png)

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.2](/figures/page-005-fig__b_2-1.png)
*Fig. B.2: The complex number \(z = a + jb\) is the point \((a, b)\) in the complex plane, with magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\) measured from the positive real axis; its conjugate \(z^* = a - jb\) is the mirror reflection of \(z\) across the real axis.*

## 2. Reading \(z = a + jb\) Correctly

Referring to Fig. B.2, the complex number \(z = a + jb\) corresponds to the point \((a, b)\) in the **complex plane**: \(a\) is the horizontal coordinate on the real axis, and \(b\) is the vertical coordinate on the imaginary axis.

The formal definitions are:

$$\operatorname{Re}(z) = a \qquad \operatorname{Im}(z) = b$$

### COMMON MISTAKE

The **imaginary part** of \(z\) is \(b\) — a plain real number. It is **not** \(jb\). The expression \(jb\) is the imaginary *term* in the algebraic form, but \(\operatorname{Im}(z)\) strips away the \(j\) and returns only the coefficient.

The **conjugate** \(z^* = a - jb\) is the mirror image of \(z\) reflected across the real axis: the real part \(a\) is unchanged, and the sign of the imaginary component is flipped.

### TOP-SCORE DISTINCTION

Keep three types of data cleanly separated:
- **Coordinate data:** the pair \((a, b)\)
- **Geometric data:** the magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\)
- **Symbolic form:** the expression \(a + jb\)

Mixing these — for example, writing the imaginary part as \(jb\) or treating \((r, \theta)\) as Cartesian coordinates — is a reliable source of avoidable mark loss.

$$z = a + jb$$
*This is the **rectangular form** of a complex number, where \(a\) is the real part and \(b\) is the imaginary part coefficient — a plain real number that scales the imaginary axis direction \(j\).*

$$\operatorname{Re}(z)=a, \qquad \operatorname{Im}(z)=b, \qquad z^*=a-jb$$
*These three notations define the real part, the imaginary part, and the complex conjugate of \(z\); note carefully that \(\operatorname{Im}(z) = b\), a real number, **not** \(jb\) — the symbol \(j\) is the axis marker and is not included in the value of the imaginary part.*

## 3. Rectangular Form, Polar Form, and Euler Form

The same point \((a, b)\) in the complex plane can be described using **polar coordinates**: \(r\) is the distance from the origin (the magnitude), and \(\theta\) is the angle measured counterclockwise from the positive real axis.

The conversion from polar to rectangular is:

$$a = r\cos\theta, \qquad b = r\sin\theta$$

Substituting into \(z = a + jb\) gives the **polar form**:

$$z = r(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta)$$

Euler's formula states that \(e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta\), so the polar form collapses into the compact **Euler form**:

$$z = re^{j\theta}$$

The teaching point is **not** three separate formulas — it is one complex number described in three coordinate languages.

### TOP-SCORE INSIGHT

A common exam error is treating rectangular data \((a, b)\) and polar data \((r, \theta)\) as if they were independent pieces of information. They are not: both describe the **same point**. Substituting \(a\) and \(b\) from one form into a formula that expects \(r\) and \(\theta\) — or vice versa — without converting is a guaranteed error.

$$a = r\cos\theta, \qquad b = r\sin\theta$$
*These equations convert the same complex-plane point from polar coordinates \((r, \theta)\) into the rectangular coordinates \((a, b)\) by projecting the radius \(r\) onto the real and imaginary axes.*

$$z = a + jb = r(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta) = re^{j\theta}$$
*Rectangular form, polar form, and Euler form are three fully equivalent representations of the same complex number — the same point in the complex plane, expressed in three different coordinate languages.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Complex numbers simplify derivations even when the problem starts and ends with real numbers.
- \(\operatorname{Im}(z) = b\), the real coefficient of \(j\) — never the full term \(jb\).
- Rectangular \(a + jb\), polar \(r(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta)\), and Euler \(re^{j\theta}\) all describe the same point.

*In the next section we will build on this notation to use complex numbers more actively in analysis.*
